one_line_takeaway: Under strict privacy constraints that block use of public cloud MLLMs, injecting human verbal salience into well-engineered prompts lets Gemini 2.5 Pro exceed both a specialized CNN baseline (MSE 0.345) and human-examiner accuracy (MSE 0.062) on iris presentation attack detection, reaching MSE 0.053, while naive short prompting alone underperforms the CNN.
key_finding: With naive short prompting (RQ1), both MLLMs underperform the CNN baseline overall (Gemini MSE 0.416, Llama MSE 0.422 vs. CNN 0.345 +/- 0.041), though Gemini beats the CNN on 4/8 attack types (contacts+print, post-mortem, printouts, textured contacts) and correctly identifies all live irises. A structured long prompt alone (RQ2) lets Gemini match/beat both baselines (MSE 0.062, equal to the human baseline's 0.062 and well below the CNN's 0.345), while Llama shows almost no improvement from the long prompt alone (MSE 0.411). Adding human salience as in-context exemplars (RQ3) improves both models in every configuration tested; the single best result in the paper is Gemini with long prompt + human salience, MSE 0.053, which is the only configuration to beat the human baseline (0.062). MESH salience (RQ4) also beats the CNN baseline in all configurations but never beats the human baseline; MESH only outperforms raw human-salience prompting in the short-prompt (poorly engineered) regime, where Gemini-generated MESH gives a bigger boost than Llama-generated MESH. A failure mode noted by the authors: for Llama, both short+Gemini-MESH and long+Gemini-MESH configurations degenerate into classifying every single image as an attack (100% BPCER on live irises, 0 on all attack classes), which they attribute to the much longer Gemini MESH prompt (~3x more tokens than Llama MESH) potentially triggering visual-attention degradation in CLIP-based vision backbones under long text context, citing prior work (Sun et al.) on this phenomenon in other MLLMs. Gemini's confidence outputs were observed to be roughly bimodal while Llama expressed more graded/intermediate confidence.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (per Fig. 1, explicitly labeled 'Paper Contribution') is threefold: (1) a novel prompt-engineering framework for iris presentation attack detection (PAD) using general-purpose multimodal LLMs (Gemini 2.5 Pro and Llama 3.2-Vision:90b) that are deployable within institutional privacy/IRB constraints (i.e., not sent to public cloud services like ChatGPT/Claude); (2) 'Machine-Expanded Saliency from Human' (MESH), a technique where an MLLM (Llama or Gemini) expands terse human verbal descriptions of iris attack indicators into comprehensive image descriptions, which are then injected into PAD prompts as in-context exemplars; and (3) an empirical evaluation across 16 experiment configurations (2 models x 8 prompt variants: short/long base prompt x {no salience, human salience, Llama MESH, Gemini MESH}) on an IRB-restricted 224-image, 7-attack-type iris dataset, benchmarked against a retrained DenseNet-121 CNN baseline (from Boyd et al.) and reported human-examiner accuracy (reused from a prior study with original authors' permission). The authors also demonstrate via UMAP visualization of SigLIP (+Gemma-fused) embeddings that pretrained vision transformers cluster iris attack types to some degree without any iris-specific training, motivating the prompt-based approach.
threat_model: Biometric presentation attacks against iris recognition/authentication systems, i.e., an adversary attempting to spoof an iris scanner via: artificial eyes/glass prostheses, printed iris photographs, printouts of irises wearing textured contacts, textured contact lenses, post-mortem (cadaver) eyes, diseased eyes, and synthetically GAN-generated iris imagery (StyleGAN2/StyleGAN3). The defender's goal is binary classification of a presented iris image as live/normal vs. attack. The deployment constraint that shapes the whole study is a privacy/compliance threat model on the defender's side: biometric images are PII under IRB and data-sharing-license restrictions that forbid transmission to public commercial MLLM APIs (ChatGPT, Claude, etc.), so only an institution-approved bilateral-agreement service (Gemini 2.5 Pro via the authors' university-Google agreement) or a fully locally-hosted model (Llama 3.2-Vision) may be used.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) cost and compliance constraints forced sub-sampling to only 224 images (30/class max, only 14 for the artificial-eye class) from a 7-attack-type set, limiting statistical power despite convergence checks; (2) only two prompting extremes were tested (minimal short prompt vs. heavily hand/Claude-engineered long prompt) with no exploration of the middle ground; (3) the human baseline was not re-collected but reused from a prior published study, so it is not a matched, contemporaneous comparison; (4) only one MLLM per class of deployment constraint was tested (one institutionally-licensed commercial model, one open-weight local model) rather than a broader model sweep, because privacy/IRB rules eliminated most commercial MLLM options from consideration; (5) no calibration or adversarial-robustness analysis was performed (flagged as future work); (6) the Llama Gemini-MESH degenerate all-attack classification failure is noted but not root-caused beyond a plausible hypothesis tied to prompt length/attention degradation, not verified experimentally in this paper.

datasets_used: 224-image IRB-restricted iris PAD test set spanning 8 classes (live iris n=30, artificial eye n=14, contacts+print n=30, diseased n=30, post-mortem n=30, printout n=30, synthetic n=30, textured contact n=30), sampled from the test set used in Boyd et al.'s prior iris PAD study

Verbal human-saliency elicitation dataset: 70 non-expert and 8 expert participants describing normal/abnormal judgments on iris images drawn from Boyd et al.'s dataset plus 100 additional StyleGAN2/StyleGAN3-generated synthetic iris samples (50 each)
quantitative_results: Gemini, Long + Human salience MSE: 0.053 (best result overall, beats human baseline)
Llama, Short + Gemini MESH MSE: 0.125 (but degenerate: classifies all images as attack)
Llama, Long + Gemini MESH MSE: 0.125 (also degenerate: classifies all images as attack)
baselines_compared: Domain-specific salience-guided CNN (DenseNet-121, trained per Boyd et al. protocol, 10 runs, leave-one-out-attack-type)
Human examiner accuracy, reused (with permission) from a prior published study, not re-collected
